pathophysiology_summary: Superficial folliculitis is inflammation/infection of the hair-follicle openings, here precipitated by shaving micro-trauma and occlusion/friction (tight clothing, heavy occlusive moisturiser), usually with Staphylococcus aureus; it presents as multiple small, follicle-centred pustules on discrete erythematous bases with normal intervening skin, minimally tender. It is benign and self-limiting with antiseptic measures. The ground-truth task is to distinguish it from the deeper/serious processes: a furuncle/carbuncle (a deep, firm, fluctuant, exquisitely tender abscess needing incision), cellulitis (confluent hot spreading tender erythema ± red streaking/fever), and herpetic folliculitis; and to manage with an antiseptic wash, avoiding occlusive products and re-shaving, withholding oral antibiotics unless there is deep/extensive/refractory infection.

differential.evidence_basis: Follicle-centred pustules in shaved/friction areas with normal intervening skin.
Screen deep firm fluctuant exquisitely tender nodules needing incision.
Screen confluent hot spreading tender erythema, red streaking, fever.
Grouped umbilicated vesicles would suggest HSV; here pustules are follicle-centred with a mechanical trigger.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Superficial folliculitis leads; deep abscess and cellulitis must be screened before conservative management.

differential.evidence_basis: Small follicle-centred pustules with normal intervening skin, no deep abscess/cellulitis/fever — superficial folliculitis.
No deep fluctuant nodules; escalation target if a boil forms.
No confluent spreading erythema/streaking/fever; escalation target if it develops.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative deep-infection screen, superficial folliculitis leads; antiseptic wash + avoid occlusives/shaving, with a deep-abscess/cellulitis safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; resolves over 7–10 days with antiseptic measures.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign superficial folliculitis.

red_flags.description: Deep/serious infection — a furuncle/carbuncle (deep firm fluctuant exquisitely tender abscess), cellulitis (confluent hot spreading tender redness, red streaking), or fever/systemic toxicity
red_flags.clinical_significance: Abscesses need incision/drainage; cellulitis needs antibiotics ± ED.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Superficial folliculitis usually needs only antiseptic/topical measures; reserve oral antibiotics for deep/extensive/refractory disease.
Occlusive products and shaving perpetuate the follicular blockage/trauma.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation for deep fluctuance/abscess and lymphadenopathy
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Antiseptic wash + topical antibiotic to larger lesions + avoid occlusives/shaving; GP/ED if a boil forms or cellulitis develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing a deep abscess or spreading cellulitis.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Superficial folliculitis: antiseptic washes (e.g. chlorhexidine) and topical measures; address mechanical triggers (shaving, friction, occlusion). Reserve oral antibiotics for deep, extensive or refractory infection; incise/drain abscesses; treat cellulitis with antibiotics.
